title: Navigating the High Cost of AI Compute
body: An analysis of compute costs and resource challenges facing generative AI companies
Source: Industry analysis on AI infrastructure economics

title: Agenda
body: Supply-demand imbalance in AI compute
Capital allocation patterns for AI startups
GPT-3 training costs and infrastructure requirements
GPU instance pricing across cloud providers
Cloud versus on-premise infrastructure decision framework
Training data scale and web resource utilization

title: The Compute Supply Crisis
body: Generative AI faces compute demand exceeding supply by approximately 10 times
This supply-demand gap has created a critical bottleneck for AI development
Access to compute resources has become a competitive differentiator in the industry

title: Compute as the Dominant Cost Factor
body: Access to compute at the lowest total cost has become a determining factor for AI companies
Compute availability and pricing directly impacts company competitiveness and viability
Infrastructure costs now drive strategic decisions for AI-focused organizations

title: Capital Allocation in AI Startups
body: Many AI startups spending over 80% of capital on compute
This extreme allocation reflects the compute-intensive nature of generative AI development
Limited capital remains for other operational needs including talent and product development

title: GPT-3 Model Scale
body: GPT-3 has 175 billion parameters
The model requires over one terabyte of memory to operate
This scale represents the magnitude of resources needed for frontier AI models

title: GPT-3 Training Cost Range
body: GPT-3 costs between $500,000 and $4.6 million per training run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs
The wide cost range reflects differences in infrastructure choices and vendor pricing
These figures represent single training runs, not including experimentation and iteration

title: A100 GPU Instance Pricing Variance
body: A100 instance pricing ranges from roughly $0.25 to $1 per hour
Nearly 4x differences exist among vendors for equivalent compute resources
Vendor selection can dramatically impact total infrastructure expenditure

title: Premium Cloud Provider Pricing
body: AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud charge premium rates for GPU instances
These major cloud providers offer comprehensive ecosystems but at higher price points
Premium pricing reflects integrated services, reliability guarantees, and enterprise support

title: Cost-Optimized Cloud Providers
body: Coreweave and Lambda Labs offer lower costs compared to major cloud providers
These specialized providers focus on GPU compute with reduced service overhead
Choosing these vendors can significantly reduce infrastructure spending for startups

title: Cloud vs. Data Center Decision Threshold
body: Startups should use cloud rather than build data centers unless infrastructure spending exceeds $50 million annually
Below this threshold, cloud services provide better economics despite per-unit premium
The $50 million breakpoint accounts for capital costs, operational overhead, and flexibility trade-offs

title: Training Data Scale
body: Training datasets already use approximately 10% of all web pages via Common Crawl
This represents a substantial portion of publicly available internet content
Data availability is becoming a constraint alongside compute resources for model training

title: Key Takeaways
body: 10x compute supply shortage creates strategic challenges for AI companies
Vendor selection and infrastructure strategy can yield 4x cost differences
Compute costs dominate startup budgets at over 80% of capital for many companies
Cloud infrastructure remains optimal for most organizations below $50 million annual spend
